sentence: Subjective:
HPI: The patient, a 60-year-old right-handed male, presents with a 2-week history of numbness and tingling in both hands, which started after a car accident in December where he broke both wrists.
He reports worsening symptoms over the last month, with increased numbness and tingling, particularly at night, and occasional pain or throbbing sensations in his hands.
He also experiences mild stiffness in his neck.
The patient denies any loss of sensation in his hands or fingers, but notes that they may be less sensitive than usual.
Past medical history: The patient has a history of hypertension, treated with Coreg, and anxiety, treated with Lexapro.
He has also had prediabetes, which he controlled through dietary changes.
He denies any history of thyroid issues.
Review of systems: The patient reports fatigue, weight gain, excessive appetite, bloating, shortness of breath, leg swelling, hunger, nailbed changes, dryness, and itching.
He also has a history of gout and muscle pain.
Current medications: Coreg for hypertension, Lexapro for anxiety.
Objective:
Vital signs: [NOT MENTIONED]
Physical exam: The patient's sensation is normal in the right hand, except for diminished pinprick sensation in the third and fourth fingers.
The sensation is decreased in the fourth and fifth fingers on the left.
Reflexes are 1+ and symmetric.
Tinel's sign is positive at both wrists and elbows.
Test Results: [NOT MENTIONED]
Assessment / Problem List:
Assessment: The patient has bilateral carpal tunnel syndrome, potentially caused by nerve irritation from past trauma.
Problem list: Carpal tunnel syndrome; cubital tunnel syndrome; prediabetes; hypertension; anxiety; gout; muscle pain.
Plan:
I prescribed a nerve test, an EMG, to determine the exact location of the nerve irritation.
I instructed the patient to continue wearing his splints and to return for a follow-up visit after the tests are complete.
If symptoms persist, we will consider a referral to the hand clinic for injections or carpal tunnel release surgery, or to an orthopedist for further treatment.
Follow-up: The patient will return for a follow-up visit after the EMG test results are available.
